section: Section 12 — Criteria for giving Legal Services
text: Every person who has to file or defend a case shall be entitled to legal services under this Act if that person is a member of a Scheduled Caste or Scheduled Tribe; a victim of trafficking in human beings or begar; a woman or a child; a person with disability; a person under circumstances of undeserved want such as being a victim of a mass disaster, ethnic violence, caste atrocity, flood, drought, earthquake or industrial disaster; an industrial workman; in custody, including custody in a protective home or in a juvenile home or in a psychiatric hospital or psychiatric nursing home; a person whose annual income does not exceed rupees three lakh. Free legal aid means: free lawyer, court fee waived, all legal expenses covered.

Lok Adalat is a forum where disputes pending in courts or at pre-litigation stage are settled amicably. Cases suitable for Lok Adalat: motor accident claims, matrimonial disputes (not divorce), labour disputes, disputes related to public utility services (electricity, water, telephone), compoundable criminal offences. Benefits of Lok Adalat: No court fee; if settled, court fee is refunded; award of Lok Adalat is final and binding, no appeal lies; settlement is quick — usually on the same day. Permanent Lok Adalats handle public utility service disputes up to Rs 1 crore. National Lok Adalat is organised by NALSA across India on specific dates.

section: Article 39A — Equal justice and free legal aid
chapter: Part IV — Directive Principles of State Policy
text: The State shall secure that the operation of the legal system promotes justice, on a basis of equal opportunity, and shall, in particular, provide free legal aid, by suitable legislation or schemes or in any other way, to ensure that opportunities for securing justice are not denied to any citizen by reason of economic or other disabilities. This article is the constitutional basis for NALSA and the free legal aid system. NALSA Helpline: 1800-11-0031 (toll-free, 24x7). NALSA operates through State Legal Services Authorities (SLSA), District Legal Services Authorities (DLSA), and Taluk Legal Services Committees (TLSC).

Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) methods available in India: (1) Mediation — neutral mediator helps parties reach agreement; faster and cheaper than court; confidential; (2) Arbitration — arbitrator gives binding decision; used mainly in commercial disputes; (3) Lok Adalat — free, fast settlement with no further appeal; (4) Conciliation — conciliator suggests settlement terms; (5) Negotiation — direct settlement between parties. Courts often refer cases to mediation before trial. Mediation centres are attached to High Courts and District Courts. The Mediation Act 2023 now gives statutory recognition to mediation settlements.

When filing a civil case in court, the plaint (written complaint) must contain: the name of the court; name, description, place of residence of plaintiff; same for defendant; facts constituting the cause of action and when it arose; facts showing the court has jurisdiction; relief claimed; value of subject matter. Court fees must be paid based on value of claim. Limitation period for civil suits: money recovery — 3 years; property recovery — 12 years; contract breach — 3 years. Always file within the limitation period or your case will be barred. For small claims up to Rs 1 lakh, file in Small Causes Court or through Lok Adalat.
